abstract: Traditional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) often faces limitations in specialized applications like biomaterials and microelectronics due to residual metal contamination. To address this, we present an all-organic, metal-free ATRP platform mediated by light and catalyzed by a phenothiazine-based photoredox catalyst. Using 10-phenylphenothiazine (PTH), we achieved controlled polymerization of various vinyl monomers, demonstrated by a linear increase in molecular weight with conversion and the production of polymers with narrow dispersities. The system exhibits high temporal control, allowing the reaction to be efficiently toggled "on" and "off" in response to light irradiation. Mechanistic studies and mass spectrometry confirm high chain-end fidelity, matching the performance of conventional metal-catalyzed systems. Furthermore, the reducing nature of the PTH catalyst enables the polymerization of monomers previously incompatible with other photoredox systems and facilitates the synthesis of well-defined block copolymers. This metal-free approach expands the scope of controlled radical polymerization, offering a versatile and sustainable synthetic route for high-purity functional materials.
